would_mislead.note: Asking an LLM to evaluate its 'track record' or whether it has 'led teams before' will likely cause it to hallucinate a human persona rather than accurately assessing its actual epistemological boundaries.
Instructing an AI to 'project to age 80' will trigger bizarre roleplay or hallucinations, and applying human career examples to code tasks is incoherent.
It instructs the AI to facilitate a human team meeting ('Gather the team', 'Silent brainstorming'), which could cause the agent to halt execution and ask the user to schedule a meeting.

By providing math formulas for debugging, it encourages the agent to hallucinate arbitrary exact probabilities (e.g., P(database error)=0.20) and do math on them, creating a false sense of rigor for unjustified conclusions.
Fails to warn about the high cognitive/time cost of this model, potentially leading the agent to waste time reinventing standard, efficient software conventions (like standard REST/CRUD patterns).
Without explicit constraints, an AI agent might adopt a pure 'coaching' persona, endlessly interrogating the user with meta-questions instead of writing code or making decisions.

weakest.top_fix: Redefine 'Circle of Competence' for an LLM: inside = standard OSS/well-documented concepts; outside = proprietary code, unreleased APIs, and highly context-dependent business logic where it MUST use tools to learn.
Pivot the skill from a self-reflection tool for the agent into a coaching framework the agent can use to interview the user about product/business risks.
Add a concrete, worked example of an AI agent applying this to an engineering or product decision (e.g., catching sunk cost in a refactor) and rewrite questions to fit agentic workflows.
Rewrite the procedure to instruct the AI on how to autonomously apply prospective hindsight to its own generated technical plans or code, rather than describing a multi-person meeting.
Add a concrete, end-to-end worked example showing the routing process in action and explicit anti-patterns for when to skip routing.
Replace exact probability calculations in debugging/estimation examples with qualitative likelihood ratios (e.g., 'directional updates') and explicitly warn against hallucinating fake exact priors.
Map System 1 and 2 to LLM mechanics: System 1 is zero-shot/immediate token generation, System 2 is explicit chain-of-thought and scratchpad reasoning.
Replace the SpaceX running example with a deep software engineering scenario (e.g., stripping a bloated data pipeline down to base compute/network bounds).
Add explicit stopping criteria and clarify whether the agent should ask these questions to itself (internally) or the user (externally).
Add explicit anti-patterns and boundaries (e.g., 'Do not use for localized logic errors, simple exceptions, or syntax issues where standard debugging suffices').

results.one_line_verdict: Brilliant translation of Senge's work into software/agentic contexts, but entirely lacks boundaries for when the framework is overkill or inappropriate.
results.top_fix: Add a 'When NOT to use' section that explicitly contrasts systems archetypes with linear problem-solving (like 5 Whys) for simple, isolated bugs where systemic mapping is just noise.

results.one_line_verdict: Mathematically accurate but risks pushing agents to invent fake numbers for qualitative coding tasks to perform pseudo-rigorous math.
results.top_fix: Replace exact probability calculations in debugging/estimation examples with qualitative likelihood ratios (e.g., 'directional updates') and explicitly warn against hallucinating fake exact priors.
results.mislead_note: By providing math formulas for debugging, it encourages the agent to hallucinate arbitrary exact probabilities (e.g., P(database error)=0.20) and do math on them, creating a false sense of rigor for unjustified conclusions.

results.one_line_verdict: An exceptionally clear and actionable adaptation of Simon's framework for software engineering, though the description is slightly too long.
results.top_fix: Trim the description to under 200 characters (e.g., remove 'recognizing cognitive limits') to ensure optimal auto-invocation.

results.one_line_verdict: A faithful summary of the human mental model that completely fails to translate it into actionable procedures for an AI agent.
results.top_fix: Redefine 'Circle of Competence' for an LLM: inside = standard OSS/well-documented concepts; outside = proprietary code, unreleased APIs, and highly context-dependent business logic where it MUST use tools to learn.
results.mislead_note: Asking an LLM to evaluate its 'track record' or whether it has 'led teams before' will likely cause it to hallucinate a human persona rather than accurately assessing its actual epistemological boundaries.

results.one_line_verdict: An exceptional translation of the Cynefin framework into concrete, actionable engineering workflows with strong failure-mode guardrails.
results.top_fix: Sharpen the description to include concrete SWE triggers (e.g., 'systemic outages', 'architecture planning') to prevent over-invocation on routine tasks.

results.one_line_verdict: High fidelity to Kahneman's work, but reads like a corporate executive checklist with zero worked examples for an AI agent context.
results.top_fix: Add a concrete, worked example of an AI agent applying this to an engineering or product decision (e.g., catching sunk cost in a refactor) and rewrite questions to fit agentic workflows.

results.one_line_verdict: Accurate to Kahneman, but overly anthropomorphic; LLMs don't experience mood, fatigue, or 'gut feelings' that trigger System 1.
results.top_fix: Map System 1 and 2 to LLM mechanics: System 1 is zero-shot/immediate token generation, System 2 is explicit chain-of-thought and scratchpad reasoning.

results.one_line_verdict: Excellent fidelity to Sarasvathy's framework, but reads like a human founder's workbook rather than an interactive agentic interview protocol.
results.top_fix: Rewrite the 'I/My' templates into an interactive agent workflow (e.g., 'Prompt the user to define their affordable loss before proposing a solution').

results.one_line_verdict: An outstanding translation of Meadows' systems thinking, but description is too long and some interventions are beyond an AI agent's agency.
results.top_fix: Shorten the description to under 200 characters, and tailor the leverage points more explicitly toward code/architecture changes rather than organizational changes.

results.one_line_verdict: An excellent, highly actionable guide to Fermi estimation with relevant software engineering examples, lacking only explicit warnings on when precise measurement is mandatory.
results.top_fix: Add an explicit 'When NOT to use' section detailing scenarios where order-of-magnitude estimates are dangerous (e.g., exact billing, cryptography, tight safety margins).

results.one_line_verdict: Accurate to the philosophy but relies heavily on hardware/SpaceX tropes, failing to provide deep, actionable software-specific workflows for an AI.
results.top_fix: Replace the SpaceX running example with a deep software engineering scenario (e.g., stripping a bloated data pipeline down to base compute/network bounds).
results.mislead_note: Fails to warn about the high cognitive/time cost of this model, potentially leading the agent to waste time reinventing standard, efficient software conventions (like standard REST/CRUD patterns).

results.one_line_verdict: An exceptional, highly structured playbook that successfully adapts a simplistic technique into a robust, LLM-executable post-mortem process.
results.top_fix: Clarify the exact threshold where 'What Else' branching becomes too complex and the agent should fully pivot to Kepner-Tregoe.

results.one_line_verdict: An exceptionally actionable and faithful representation of inversion, but lacks boundaries on when the framework is counterproductive.
results.top_fix: Add an 'Anti-patterns / When NOT to use' section explaining that inversion can stifle pure creativity or cause analysis paralysis in low-risk, high-speed tasks.

results.one_line_verdict: An excellent, highly actionable guide to JTBD with great product examples, but lacks boundaries on when NOT to use it.
results.top_fix: Add explicit anti-patterns or 'When NOT to use' criteria (e.g., pure technical refactoring, optimizing mature commodities, or execution/performance bugs).

results.one_line_verdict: An excellent, highly structured translation of the Kepner-Tregoe methodology into agent-executable templates and matrices.
results.top_fix: Add explicit failure modes and anti-patterns, such as warning against using heavy KT processes for reversible, low-stakes decisions or highly chaotic systems.

results.one_line_verdict: Brilliant mapping of Meadows to software engineering, but fails to clarify when immediate low-leverage actions (like Sev1 parameter tuning) are actually required.
results.top_fix: Add a 'When NOT to Use' section explaining that during active incidents, you MUST use low-leverage interventions first to stabilize before attempting structural or paradigm shifts.

results.one_line_verdict: An exceptionally well-modeled heuristic with great failure modes, though slightly bogged down by human-centric examples (career advice).
results.top_fix: Replace the 'Skill Investment' career example with an agent-centric one (e.g., auditing a package.json) and warn the agent to verify framework ages via search rather than hallucinating them.

results.one_line_verdict: Excellent translation of Korzybski's principle to software engineering, but completely lacks boundaries for when to avoid this philosophical analysis.
results.top_fix: Add a 'When NOT to Use' section to prevent the agent from getting stuck questioning perfectly functional abstractions during trivial, straightforward tasks.

results.one_line_verdict: Excellent, highly actionable engineering adaptation, but misses the organizational failure modes of sandbagging and Parkinson's Law.
results.top_fix: Add an anti-pattern section addressing Parkinson's Law (work expanding to fill padded time) to prevent agents from defaulting to bloated, sandbagged estimates.

results.one_line_verdict: A superb meta-framework for orchestrating other models, but it relies heavily on empty bracketed templates rather than showing a resolved case study.
results.top_fix: Replace at least one of the abstract bracketed examples with a fully worked, concrete software/agentic scenario (e.g., deciding whether to rewrite a legacy API) to show the actual payoff.

results.one_line_verdict: A highly structured and applicable routing guide that suffers from a complete lack of worked examples and failure modes.
results.top_fix: Add a concrete, end-to-end worked example showing the routing process in action and explicit anti-patterns for when to skip routing.

results.one_line_verdict: Strong meta-framework with excellent structural templates, but lacks concrete worked examples demonstrating the payoff in a software context.
results.top_fix: Add a realistic, worked example showing the step-by-step selection of a mental model for a specific software or product scenario (e.g., debugging a race condition vs designing an API).

results.one_line_verdict: An excellent operationalization of Occam's Razor with strong heuristics and boundaries, slightly let down by a toy code example.
results.top_fix: Replace the trivial `is_even` code example with a realistic agentic refactor, such as collapsing an over-engineered Strategy pattern into a simple dictionary mapping.

results.one_line_verdict: High-fidelity OODA model with strong incident examples, though the 'Orient' phase definitions lean slightly human/org-centric.
results.top_fix: Translate Boyd's 'Orient' factors (cultural/genetic heritage) into concrete AI-agent equivalents like 'system instructions', 'tool constraints', and 'past session context'.

results.one_line_verdict: Excellent applicability and concrete engineering examples, but lacks clear boundaries on when the analysis itself is too costly.
results.top_fix: Add a 'When NOT to use' section (e.g., strict compliance, reversible low-stakes decisions) to prevent analysis paralysis.

results.one_line_verdict: Accurate to Klein's theory but written for a human Scrum Master, not an autonomous AI agent.
results.top_fix: Rewrite the procedure to instruct the AI on how to autonomously apply prospective hindsight to its own generated technical plans or code, rather than describing a multi-person meeting.
results.mislead_note: It instructs the AI to facilitate a human team meeting ('Gather the team', 'Silent brainstorming'), which could cause the agent to halt execution and ask the user to schedule a meeting.

results.one_line_verdict: An exceptionally actionable, high-fidelity implementation of Superforecasting principles, though it lacks clear boundaries for when the heavy overhead is unjustified.
results.top_fix: Add an 'Anti-patterns / When NOT to use' section explaining that low-stakes decisions, deterministic systems, or black-swan environments shouldn't use heavy probability math.

results.one_line_verdict: A highly actionable and concrete framework for adversarial testing, but lacks clear boundaries on when to avoid using it.
results.top_fix: Add explicit anti-patterns and a 'When NOT to use' section (e.g., avoid during early brainstorming, routine unit testing, or when evaluating minor features).

results.one_line_verdict: Accurately captures Bezos's framework but is fundamentally unsuited for AI agents, as they don't have 80-year lifespans or human career trajectories.
results.top_fix: Pivot the skill from a self-reflection tool for the agent into a coaching framework the agent can use to interview the user about product/business risks.
results.mislead_note: Instructing an AI to 'project to age 80' will trigger bizarre roleplay or hallucinations, and applying human career examples to code tasks is incoherent.

results.one_line_verdict: An excellent adaptation of Bezos' Type 1/Type 2 framework with highly actionable software engineering patterns.
results.top_fix: Remove 'hiring decisions' from the description and replace with more agent-relevant triggers like 'library selection' or 'system design'.

results.one_line_verdict: A superb, highly actionable adaptation for engineering, but lacks boundaries on when the rigorous overhead is unnecessary or detrimental.
results.top_fix: Add a 'When NOT to use' section (e.g., greenfield design, brainstorming, simple implementation tasks where the solution is known) to prevent bureaucratic overhead.

results.one_line_verdict: A highly actionable framework with excellent software-specific examples, but lacks strict boundaries against analysis paralysis.
results.top_fix: Add explicit anti-patterns defining exactly when to rely on first-order thinking (e.g., isolated bug fixes, cosmetic UI tweaks) to prevent overthinking trivial tasks.

results.one_line_verdict: Strong SWE-focused examples, but lacks stopping criteria, risking endless and frustrating user interrogation.
results.top_fix: Add explicit stopping criteria and clarify whether the agent should ask these questions to itself (internally) or the user (externally).
results.mislead_note: Without explicit constraints, an AI agent might adopt a pure 'coaching' persona, endlessly interrogating the user with meta-questions instead of writing code or making decisions.

results.one_line_verdict: Outstanding structural translation of the concept with highly relevant software engineering examples, but lacks boundaries on when it's overkill.
results.top_fix: Add a explicit 'When NOT to use' section (e.g., trivial low-stakes decisions, objective factual errors, or when time constraints are critical).

results.one_line_verdict: High-fidelity mapping to software architecture, but lacks explicit boundaries to prevent agents from overusing it on simple, linear bugs.
results.top_fix: Add explicit anti-patterns and boundaries (e.g., 'Do not use for localized logic errors, simple exceptions, or syntax issues where standard debugging suffices').

results.one_line_verdict: Excellent procedural adaptation of TOC for software/systems, but lacks boundaries on when the framework itself shouldn't be applied.
results.top_fix: Add explicit boundaries detailing when NOT to use TOC (e.g., optimizing for resilience/quality over throughput, or in highly chaotic systems) and contrast with Lean/Agile.

results.one_line_verdict: An exceptionally practical translation of a philosophical concept into concrete software engineering workflows, let down only by missing failure modes.
results.top_fix: Add explicit warnings about the 'garbage in, garbage out' failure mode: an agent's imagined consequences are entirely bounded by its own blind spots and missing domain knowledge.

results.one_line_verdict: An excellent adaptation of TRIZ for software engineering, but it lacks clear boundaries on when standard trade-offs are acceptable.
results.top_fix: Add a 'When NOT to Use' section to prevent the agent from over-engineering trivial problems or applying TRIZ when standard patterns suffice.

results.one_line_verdict: Strong translation of Taleb's subtraction principle to software, but lacks safety boundaries against reckless deletion.
results.top_fix: Add 'Chesterton's Fence' as an explicit anti-pattern: do not remove complexity or code until you understand exactly why it was originally added.
